I’ve found that the nobility is much easier to roast when their planetary estates are literally being swallowed by a singularity.
My latest set about the inefficiency of the Imperial bureaucracy only landed well because the theater’s oxygen scrubbers failed halfway through the punchline.
There is a certain dark magic in watching a decadent governor tremble during my monologue while the rebel fleet enters the upper atmosphere.
I make a killing performing for the desperate remnants of the void-fleets, who will trade their last fuel cells for ten minutes of forgetting they are drifting toward oblivion.
It is remarkably easy to write satire about the "Eternal Emperor" when the holographic transmissions are glitching and the palace guards have already deserted their posts.
I keep scanning the boardroom for a punchline, but these executives are delivering the most deadpan performance of their lives regarding the asset liquidation.
I’ve spent ten minutes trying to find the comedic timing in the CEO's speech about "synergistic alignment," but the silence in the room is far too heavy for a joke to land.
As I watch the lawyers argue over the intellectual property clauses, I can't help but think this entire negotiation is just a high-budget sketch about corporate greed.
I tried to break the tension during the final signing with a quick observation about the catering, but the CFO looked at me as if I had just suggested we burn the headquarters down.
The sheer absurdity of two billion-dollar entities fighting over a shared logo is the kind of observational gold that usually takes me a month to write, yet here it is happening in real time.
I tried to ease the tension over the stolen mammoth tusk by mimicking the chief's indignant dance, but the look on his face suggests I might be the next sacrifice.
While the two clans argue over the boundary of the river, I find it hilarious that they are fighting over a patch of mud that washes away every full moon.
I told a joke about the rival tribe's clumsy spear-throwing, and for a moment, the war drums stopped just so everyone could laugh at their misfortune.
It is difficult to land a punchline when the audience is more interested in sharpening their flint axes than listening to my observations on cave paintings.
I attempted to mediate the dispute by pretending to be a spirit from the Great Void, but my impression was so convincing that they started arguing about which rock to offer me.
I perform my tight five for the central traffic grid, timing my punchlines to the rhythmic pulse of the automated commuters.
It is a challenge to find irony in a society where the skyscrapers can literally rewrite their own architecture to suit the mood of the crowd.
I spent an hour trying to roast the city's subconscious, but the sentient sewage system just countered with a joke about my biological fragility.
My latest set focuses on the absurdity of holographic billboards that try to sell me nostalgia for a century they never actually witnessed.
I love watching the neon districts flicker in rhythmic bursts of laughter whenever I nail a joke about the city's obsession with optimizing its own joy.
I tried to lighten the mood with a few tight five minutes, but the judge is a three-headed hydra who doesn't seem to appreciate observational humor about the afterlife.
My defense attorney is a literal ghost, which makes it incredibly difficult to coordinate our timing for the punchlines during the cross-examination.
I keep trying to read the room, but it is hard to gauge the crowd's energy when the jury consists of silent, floating eyeballs from the fifth dimension.
The prosecution is attempting to frame my career as a series of felonies, but I maintain that roasting a deity is a protected form of artistic expression.
I am currently awaiting my sentence in a holding cell made of solidified screams, though I'm already drafting a hilarious bit about the lack of amenities.
I’ve tried telling my best tight-five to the leaking bulkheads, but the only thing laughing back is the sound of the hull groaning under ten thousand pounds of pressure.
The flickering emergency lights provide a mood that is less "intimate club" and more "looming psychological breakdown," which really kills my timing.
I’m performing for a crowd of bioluminescent mold and rusted terminals, and frankly, their silence is the toughest room I’ve ever played.
There is something poetically tragic about practicing my crowd work in a flooded galley where the only one listening is a floating, waterlogged manual on deep-sea survival.
I keep waiting for the crushing weight of the ocean to provide a punchline, but so far, the cosmic irony of dying in a sunken lab is just a bit too on the nose.
I stare at this blank white canvas and realize the punchline is that we are all paying twenty dollars to look at nothing.
This interactive sound installation is essentially just a very expensive way to make a fart noise in a quiet room.
I find it hilarious that the artist describes this pile of rusted scrap metal as a meditation on grief when it looks exactly like my first car.
I am trying to figure out if the man standing perfectly still in the corner is a piece of performance art or if he has simply fallen asleep.
The way these critics whisper in hushed, reverent tones about a banana taped to a wall is the greatest piece of improvisational comedy I have ever witnessed.
I find that the sight of a plague doctor’s beak is the perfect prop for a joke about poultry, provided the audience isn't already coughing up blood.
My latest routine regarding the inevitability of the Great Mortality is killing the crowd, though I suspect it is the fleas doing most of the heavy lifting.
It is remarkably easy to get a laugh when you remind the nobility that the boils on their skin look exactly like the warts on a peasant's backside.
I’ve noticed that the frantic ringing of the death bells provides a wonderful rhythmic accompaniment to my timing during a punchline.
There is a certain dark irony in performing for a room full of terrified sinners who are suddenly very willing to pay for a distraction from their impending doom.
I tried to kill with a punchline, but the audience froze in a perfect loop, repeating the same three seconds of laughter for an eternity.
I noticed the crowd's applause sounded like a corrupted audio file, stuttering in a rhythmic glitch that actually improved my timing.
I paused for a dramatic beat and watched as the brick wall behind me dissolved into a cascade of neon green binary code.
My set was going great until I looked down and realized my microphone had merged with my hand in a jagged, polygonal clipping error.
I asked the front row if they were having a good time, only to realize they were all identical clones with the same blank, unrendered faces.
